title: Mean and standard deviation of a binomial random variable
description: Practice calculating the mean and standard deviation of a binomial random variable.
topic_path: Binomial mean and standard deviation formulas
Mean and standard deviation of a binomial random variable

assessment_items.question_content:
A company produces processing chips for cell phones. At one of its large factories, $2\%$ of the chips produced are defective in some way. A quality check involves randomly selecting and testing $500$ chips. 

**What are the mean and standard deviation of the number of defective processing chips in these samples?**  
*You may round your answers to the nearest tenth.*

mean: [[☃ numeric-input 1]] chips

standard deviation: [[☃ numeric-input 2]] chips
hints:
For a binomial random variable $X$, we can use this formula for mean: 

$\mu_X=np$

We use this formula for the standard deviation:

$\sigma_X=\sqrt{np(1-p)}$

Since we are randomly selecting $500$ chips, we have $n=500$.

There is a $2\%$ chance that any given chip is defective, so we have $p=0.02$.

The answer:

mean: $10$ chips

standard deviation: $3.1$ chips

assessment_items.question_content:
Courtney makes $80\%$ of the free throws she attempts. She often stays after practice to shoot sets of $10$ free throws. Assume that the results of free throws are independent from each other.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of the number of free throws she makes in each set of $10$?**  
*You may round your answers to the nearest tenth.*

mean: [[☃ numeric-input 1]] free throws

standard deviation: [[☃ numeric-input 2]] free throws
hints:
For a binomial random variable $X$, we can use this formula for mean: 

$\mu_X=np$

We use this formula for the standard deviation:

$\sigma_X=\sqrt{np(1-p)}$

Since each set consists of $10$ free throw attempts, we have $n=10$.

We were told that she makes $80\%$ of these attempts, so we have $p=0.80$.

$\begin{align}
\mu_X &= np \\\\
&=(10)(0.80) \\\\
&=8 \text{ free throws} \\\\\\
\sigma_X &= \sqrt{np(1-p)} \\\\
&=\sqrt{(10)(0.80)(1-0.80)} \\\\
&= \sqrt{1.6}\\\\
&\approx 1.265 \text{ free throws}
\end{align}$

The answer:

mean: $8$ free throws

standard deviation: $1.3$ free throws

assessment_items.question_content:
Ms. Davis is doing an activity with her statistics students where she gives them a $20$-question multiple choice test, and they know none of the answers. Students need to guess on every question, and each question has $5$ possible choices, $1$ of which is correct.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of the number of questions that each student gets correct?**  
*You may round your answers to the nearest tenth.*

mean: [[☃ numeric-input 1]] correct

standard deviation: [[☃ numeric-input 2]] correct
hints:
For a binomial random variable $X$, we can use this formula for mean: 

$\mu_X=np$

We use this formula for the standard deviation:

$\sigma_X=\sqrt{np(1-p)}$

Since there are $20$ questions, we have $n=20$.

Each question has $5$ possible choices, $1$ of which is correct,  so we have $p=\dfrac15=0.20$.

$\begin{align}
\mu_X &= np \\\\
&=(20)(0.20) \\\\
&=4 \text{ correct} \\\\\\
\sigma_X &= \sqrt{np(1-p)} \\\\
&=\sqrt{(20)(0.20)(1-0.20)} \\\\
&= \sqrt{3.2}\\\\
&\approx 1.79 \text{ correct}
\end{align}$

The answer:

mean: $4$ correct

standard deviation: $1.8$ correct

assessment_items.question_content:
A television show tests the abilities of alleged psychics by presenting contestants with a set of $4$ cards placed upside down, $1$ of which has a star printed on it. Each contestant  attempts to identify which card has the star on it for a series of $50$ trials.

**Assuming that the contestants are purely guessing, what are the mean and standard deviation of the number of trials where the contestant guesses correctly?**  
*You may round your answers to the nearest tenth.*

mean: [[☃ numeric-input 1]] correct

standard deviation: [[☃ numeric-input 2]] correct
hints:
For a binomial random variable $X$, we can use this formula for mean: 

$\mu_X=np$

We use this formula for the standard deviation:

$\sigma_X=\sqrt{np(1-p)}$

Since each contestant does a series of $50$ trials, we have $n=50$.

There $4$ cards in each trial, $1$ of which is correct,  so we have $p=\dfrac14=0.25$.

The answer:

mean: $12.5$ correct

standard deviation: $3.1$ correct

assessment_items.question_content:
A division of a company has over $200$ employees, $40\%$ of which are male. Each month, the company randomly selects $8$ of these employees to have lunch with the CEO.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of the number of males selected each month?**  
*You may round your answers to the nearest tenth.*

mean: [[☃ numeric-input 1]] males

standard deviation: [[☃ numeric-input 2]] males
hints:
For a binomial random variable $X$, we can use this formula for mean: 

$\mu_X=np$

We use this formula for the standard deviation:

$\sigma_X=\sqrt{np(1-p)}$

Since they select $8$ employees each month, we have $n=8$.

We know $40\%$ of the employees are male,  so we have $p=0.40$.

The answer:

mean: $3.2$ males

standard deviation: $1.4$ males

assessment_items.question_content:
A college has over $5{,}000$ students, $10\%$ of which are left-handed. A new lecture hall is being planned that will seat $300$ students, and college officials want to be sure there are enough left-handed desks. Suppose we randomly select groups of $300$ students from this college.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of the number of left-handed students in each group of $300$?**  
*You may round your answers to the nearest tenth.*

mean: [[☃ numeric-input 1]] students

standard deviation: [[☃ numeric-input 2]] students
hints:
For a binomial random variable $X$, we can use this formula for mean: 

$\mu_X=np$

We use this formula for the standard deviation:

$\sigma_X=\sqrt{np(1-p)}$

Since we are considering randomly chosen groups of $300$ students, we have $n=300$.

We know $10\%$ of the students are left-handed,  so we have $p=0.10$.

$\begin{align}
\mu_X &= np \\\\
&=(300)(0.10) \\\\
&=30 \text{ students} \\\\\\
\sigma_X &= \sqrt{np(1-p)} \\\\
&=\sqrt{(300)(0.10)(1-0.10)} \\\\
&= \sqrt{27}\\\\
&\approx 5.196 \text{ students}
\end{align}$

The answer:

mean: $30$ students

standard deviation: $5.2$ students

assessment_items.question_content:
In a game of luck, a turn consists of a player rolling $12$ fair $6$-sided dice. Let $X=$ the number of dice that land showing "$1$" in a turn.

**Find the mean and standard deviation of $X$.**  
*You may round your answers to the nearest tenth.*

$\mu_X=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] dice

$\sigma_X\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] dice
hints:
For a binomial random variable $X$, we can use this formula for mean: 

$\mu_X=np$

We use this formula for the standard deviation:

$\sigma_X=\sqrt{np(1-p)}$

Since a turn consists of rolling $12$ dice, we have $n=12$.

The dice are fair and $6$-sided,  so the probability that each die lands showing "$1$" is $p=\dfrac16$.

$\begin{align}
\mu_X &= np \\\\
&=(12)\left(\dfrac16\right) \\\\
&=2 \text{ dice} \\\\\\
\sigma_X &= \sqrt{np(1-p)} \\\\
&=\sqrt{(12)\left(\dfrac16\right)\left(1-\dfrac16\right)} \\\\
&\approx \sqrt{1.667}\\\\
&\approx 1.291 \text{ dice}
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\mu_X= 2$ dice

$\sigma_X\approx1.3$ dice

assessment_items.question_content:
$60\%$ of a certain species of tomato live after transplanting from pot to garden. Eli transplants $16$ of these tomato plants in a garden each month. Assume that the plants live independently of each other. Let $X =$ the number of tomato plants that live each month.

**Find the mean and standard deviation of $X$.**  
*You may round your answers to the nearest tenth.*

$\mu_X=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] tomatoes

$\sigma_X\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] tomatoes
hints:
For a binomial random variable $X$, we can use this formula for mean: 

$\mu_X=np$

We use this formula for the standard deviation:

$\sigma_X=\sqrt{np(1-p)}$

Since Eli transplants $16$ plants each month, we have $n=16$.

We know that $60\%$ live after transplant, so  $p=0.60$.

$\begin{align}
\mu_X &= np \\\\
&=(16)\left(0.60\right) \\\\
&=9.6 \text{ tomatoes} \\\\\\
\sigma_X &= \sqrt{np(1-p)} \\\\
&=\sqrt{(16)\left(0.60\right)\left(1-0.60\right)} \\\\
&= \sqrt{3.84}\\\\
&\approx 1.9596 \text{ tomatoes}
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\mu_X= 9.6$ tomatoes

$\sigma_X\approx2.0$ tomatoes

assessment_items.question_content:
A blood plasma donation company accepts $40$ volunteers per day. Based on previous data, they know that $15\%$ of volunteers will be turned away for some reason. Let $X$ represent the number of patients that get turned away in a set of $40$ volunteers.

**Find the mean and standard deviation of $X$.**  
*You may round your answers to the nearest tenth.*

$\mu_X=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] volunteers

$\sigma_X\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] volunteers
hints:
For a binomial random variable $X$, we can use this formula for mean: 

$\mu_X=np$

We use this formula for the standard deviation:

$\sigma_X=\sqrt{np(1-p)}$

Since we are interested in sets of $40$ volunteers, we have $n=40$.

We know that $15\%$ of volunteers get turned away, so  $p=0.15$.

$\begin{align}
\mu_X &= np \\\\
&=(40)\left(0.15\right) \\\\
&=6 \text{ volunteers} \\\\\\
\sigma_X &= \sqrt{np(1-p)} \\\\
&=\sqrt{(40)\left(0.15\right)\left(1-0.15\right)} \\\\
&= \sqrt{5.1}\\\\
&\approx 2.258 \text{ volunteers}
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\mu_X= 6$ volunteers

$\sigma_X\approx2.3$ volunteers

assessment_items.question_content:
A candy machine contains over $1{,}000$ pieces of candy, $30\%$ of which are blue. Customers get an [[☃ definition 1]] of $15$ candies in a purchase. Let $X=$ the number of blue candies that a random customer gets in a purchase.

**Find the mean and standard deviation of $X$.**  
*You may round your answers to the nearest tenth.*

$\mu_X=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] candies

$\sigma_X\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] candies
hints:
For a binomial random variable $X$, we can use this formula for mean: 

$\mu_X=np$

We use this formula for the standard deviation:

$\sigma_X=\sqrt{np(1-p)}$

Since there are $15$ candies in a purchase, we have $n=15$.

We know that $30\%$ of the candies are blue, so  $p=0.30$.

$\begin{align}
\mu_X &= np \\\\
&=(15)\left(0.30\right) \\\\
&=4.5 \text{ candies} \\\\\\
\sigma_X &= \sqrt{np(1-p)} \\\\
&=\sqrt{(15)\left(0.30\right)\left(1-0.30\right)} \\\\
&= \sqrt{3.15}\\\\
&\approx 1.775 \text{ candies}
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\mu_X= 4.5$ candies

$\sigma_X\approx1.8$ candies

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A large fast-food chain runs a promotion where $1$-in-$4$ boxes of french fries include a coupon for a free box of french fries. Suppose that some location sells $100$ of these boxes of fries per day. Let $X=$ the number of coupons won per day.

**Find the mean and standard deviation of $X$.**  
*You may round your answers to the nearest tenth.*

$\mu_X=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] coupons

$\sigma_X\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] coupons
hints:
For a binomial random variable $X$, we can use this formula for mean: 

$\mu_X=np$

We use this formula for the standard deviation:

$\sigma_X=\sqrt{np(1-p)}$

Since they sell $100$ of these boxes per day, we have $n=100$.

We know that $1$-in-$4$ of the boxes include a coupon, so  $p=\dfrac14=0.25$.

$\begin{align}
\mu_X &= np \\\\
&=(100)\left(0.25\right) \\\\
&=25 \text{ coupons} \\\\\\
\sigma_X &= \sqrt{np(1-p)} \\\\
&=\sqrt{(100)\left(0.25\right)\left(1-0.25\right)} \\\\
&= \sqrt{18.75}\\\\
&\approx 4.330 \text{ coupons}
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\mu_X= 25$ coupons

$\sigma_X\approx4.3$ coupons

assessment_items.question_content:
An egg supplier to a restaurant sends a daily shipment of $500$ eggs. Previous records show that $4\%$ of these eggs arrive damaged to the restaurant. Let $X=$ the number of eggs that arrive damaged on a randomly selected day.

**Find the mean and standard deviation of $X$.**  
*You may round your answers to the nearest tenth.*

$\mu_X=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] eggs

$\sigma_X\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] eggs
hints:
For a binomial random variable $X$, we can use this formula for mean: 

$\mu_X=np$

We use this formula for the standard deviation:

$\sigma_X=\sqrt{np(1-p)}$

Since they ship $500$ eggs each day, we have $n=500$.

We know that $4\%$ of eggs arrive damaged, so  $p=0.04$.

$\begin{align}
\mu_X &= np \\\\
&=(500)\left(0.04\right) \\\\
&=20 \text{ eggs} \\\\\\
\sigma_X &= \sqrt{np(1-p)} \\\\
&=\sqrt{(500)\left(0.04\right)\left(1-0.04\right)} \\\\
&= \sqrt{19.2}\\\\
&\approx 4.382 \text{ eggs}
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\mu_X= 20$ eggs

$\sigma_X\approx4.4$ eggs
